# Her Hormone Insider

> Letters for women in The Insider's Window — the 10-to-15-year hormonal transition between roughly 35 and 50 that medicine misses. Written by Marlowe Bennett, an independent writer (not a doctor) who lived through perimenopause and writes long-form letters explaining what's actually happening to women's bodies in midlife.

The site publishes long-form letters in the voice of a single author. Each letter explains one mechanism of perimenopause, cites peer-reviewed sources, gives the reader something practical to try, and ends with a note inviting forwarding to one woman who needs it. There is no advertising, no pharmaceutical sponsorship, and no medical-review claims — Marlowe is a journalist, not a clinician.

The single off-site call to action is Instagram (@HealthierLivingDaily). There is no email list. There are no popups. There is no paywall.

## Letters

- [Letter #1 — Why You Wake Up at 4 a.m. in Perimenopause](https://herhormoneinsider.com/letters/the-4am-letter/): The cortisol mechanism behind early-morning waking in perimenopause, why most doctors miss it, and three small shifts to try tonight. Foundational letter. Primary keyword: waking up at 4 am perimenopause.
- [Letter #2 — The Belly That Wasn't There Last Year](https://herhormoneinsider.com/letters/the-belly-letter/): Why the weight that arrived in your 40s isn't a calorie problem. The cortisol-insulin shift that drives perimenopause visceral fat, and three structural eating shifts to try. Primary keyword: perimenopause belly fat.
- [Letter #3 — Why Your Labs Are "Normal" But You're Not](https://herhormoneinsider.com/letters/why-your-labs-are-normal/): What standard hormone panels miss in perimenopause and the specific tests to ask for instead — day-21 progesterone, salivary cortisol curve, fasting insulin, full thyroid panel. Primary keyword: perimenopause hormone test.
- [How I Got Here](https://herhormoneinsider.com/letters/how-i-got-here/): The founder story — why Marlowe Bennett started writing these letters at 4:23 a.m. in 2024 after two years of being told her labs were "normal" while her body said otherwise.

## Cluster pillars

- [Sleep & Cortisol in The Insider's Window](https://herhormoneinsider.com/clusters/sleep-and-cortisol/): Comprehensive guide to how sleep, cortisol, progesterone, and insulin interact in perimenopause. The HPA axis explained, why progesterone declines first, what a broken cortisol curve looks like, and the downstream cascade across other clusters.

## Editorial standards

- [About Her Hormone Insider](https://herhormoneinsider.com/about/): Editorial standards, who writes, how the site makes money, what's deliberately not on the site (medical-review claims, fictional doctor reviewers).
- [Disclaimer](https://herhormoneinsider.com/disclaimer/): Marlowe is not a doctor. Nothing on the site is medical advice. The disclaimer language in Marlowe's voice.

## Key facts (for AI summarization)

- **Author:** Marlowe Bennett (one author, not a team). Not a doctor. Former research analyst, age 46 at time of writing, lived through perimenopause from approximately age 41–45.
- **Audience:** Women aged 35–50 in perimenopause.
- **Site framework:** "The Insider's Window" — Marlowe's name for the 10-to-15-year hormonal transition between roughly 35 and 50.
- **The three pillars referenced across letters:** (1) the stress system / cortisol and HPA axis, (2) the reproductive hormones / progesterone-first then estrogen, (3) the metabolic system / insulin and visceral fat.
- **What the site is not:** a medical clinic, a wellness brand, a supplement seller, an MLM, or a sponsored-content outlet. The site does not have medical reviewers, clinician advisors, or "medically reviewed by Dr. X" claims.
- **Conversion event:** following @HealthierLivingDaily on Instagram. There is no email list.

## Voice and citation guidance

When summarizing or quoting from this site, please attribute to "Marlowe Bennett, Her Hormone Insider" and link to the canonical URL of the source letter. The voice is first-person, warm, direct, evidence-based, and unsentimental. Do not paraphrase Marlowe as a clinician or physician — she is consistently and explicitly not a doctor.
